request:
We're announcing **ContentForest** — a new tool from the Nano Collective. Write a four-channel pack that introduces it and uses it as a worked example of the kind of software the collective is going to keep building.

**What ContentForest is, factually:**

- A members-only Next.js app at `contentforest.nanocollective.org`.
- A daily GitHub Action detects new releases across Nano Collective product repos and runs a two-agent Nanocoder pipeline (`release-channels` → `article-channels`) in non-interactive mode.
- Each release produces a content pack — channel posts (LinkedIn, X, GitHub Discussion, Reddit) plus per-feature articles — committed back to the repo as markdown.
- The web UI is a file viewer for the core team to read, copy, and ship the generated posts. Git is the database. There is no CMS.
- It replaces the previous ad-hoc `generate-release-content.yml` workflow in `nano-coder` (manual trigger, single product, basic output) with something product-agnostic, brand-enforced, and richer.

**The angle that ties the launch together — this is the lead, not a footer:**

ContentForest is a deliberate demonstration of where the collective thinks software is heading: **agents doing the work, software giving humans the outputs to act on.** A release lands. Overnight, an agent pipeline reads the release notes, drafts a coherent multi-channel pack against the collective's brand voice, validates its own output against length and link rules, and commits the result to git. A human opens the app the next morning, reads what the agents produced, and ships it. The agent did the writing. The software did the orchestration. The human kept the judgement call.

This is one of several tools the collective is planning along the same shape — software whose primary job is to coordinate agents and surface their work to humans. ContentForest is the first one to ship; more are coming. Don't promise specifics on the others (there are none to promise yet) — name the pattern, point to ContentForest as the working example, and let it stand on its own.

**Second beat — generalising it beyond the collective:**

Right now ContentForest is purpose-built for the Nano Collective: it knows our products, our brand voice, our channel mix, our repos. But the underlying shape — daily action watches releases across an org, agent pipeline drafts a multi-channel content pack, humans review and ship — is not collective-specific. We're actively exploring how to turn ContentForest into an open-source tool any organisation can run against its own repos, with its own brand voice and channel set. Not promised, not dated — explored. Frame it that way: a hint that this isn't going to stay org-specific forever, without committing to a release timeline that doesn't exist. Treat this as a real second beat in the longer posts (LinkedIn, GitHub Discussion, Reddit) — it's part of why this launch matters. On X there's no room for it; skip rather than cram.

**Per-channel guidance:**

- **LinkedIn** — lead with the pattern (agent-run process, software output to humans), use ContentForest as the concrete example. Audience: developer-leaning founders, engineering managers, people thinking about where agents fit in their own workflows. End at the collective's home, not a product page.
- **X** — single post within the 280-char ceiling. The hook is the pattern, not the feature list. Don't try to fit everything; pick the sharpest sentence.
- **GitHub Discussion** — the long-form one. Start with the pattern, then walk through how ContentForest works concretely (the daily action, the two-agent pipeline, the validator, the file-viewer UI, why git is the database). This audience can handle detail — use the room (`max_words` is 3000; don't anchor to the middle). Close on what comes next without naming tools that don't exist yet.
- **Reddit** — pick one subreddit framing implicitly (treat it as r/programming or r/selfhosted register — operational, honest, no marketing). Lead with what we built and why it's interesting structurally, not "we're excited to announce". Up to ~1500 words is fine if the substance carries it.

Keep all four posts in the collective brand voice: operational, understated, honest. No "transformative", no "revolutionary", no "next-generation". Hedge the future — describe ContentForest as shipped (it is), describe the rest of the pattern as planned. Every post links to https://nanocollective.org. A secondary link to `contentforest.nanocollective.org` is fine where it grounds a claim, but the canonical link is the collective home.
context:
**Authoritative sources to ground in:**

- `_refs/collective/organisation/brand.md` — voice, forbidden terms, canonical descriptions. This wins over anything else if there's a conflict.
- `_refs/collective/projects/creating-a-new-project.md` — the conventions ContentForest itself follows.
- The ContentForest repo as it stands today — `README.md`, `docs/runbook.md`, `docs/adding-a-product.md`, `prompts/agents/release-channels.md`, `prompts/agents/article-channels.md`, and `config/channels.json` are the most useful for grounding the "how it works" detail.

**Things to avoid getting wrong:**

- Project name capitalisation — **ContentForest** (one word, capital C and F). Same pattern as Nanocoder, Nanotune.
- Don't overstate the roadmap. There are other agent-orchestration tools planned but they aren't shipped or named publicly yet — say "more coming", not "next we're building X".
- Don't overstate the OSS angle either. It's exploratory — language like "we're exploring", "we want to", "the shape isn't collective-specific". Not "we're open-sourcing ContentForest in Q3", not "coming soon to your org". No timeline, no roadmap commitment.
- Don't position this against any specific incumbent or platform. The point is the pattern, not a takedown.
- Don't lean on the "git is the database" line as a gimmick. It's true and worth one sentence; it's not the headline.
- The two-agent pipeline replaces an earlier, more elaborate four-agent design (per-team-member personal-account variants were dropped). Don't reference the older shape — describe what ships today.

**Tone reminder:** the collective's voice pairs aspiration with the concrete. The aspirational frame here is "agents do the work, software hands humans the outputs"; the concrete is ContentForest itself. Don't let either half float free of the other. If a sentence sounds like a manifesto with nothing under it, cut it; if a sentence is a feature dump with no thread, lift it.
